docs.title: adoy
docs.text: An agent-first static site generator — build and manage your site through natural language.

docs.title: Features
docs.text:
• Incremental builds — only changed content, templates, or config triggers a rebuild. Large sites stay fast.
  • Multiple content types — define sections with independent templates and permalink patterns.
  • Taxonomies — aggregate content across sections with tags, categories, series, or any custom grouping.
  • Agent-first — every project includes a CLAUDE.md so an AI agent can manage content and templates from natural language instructions.

docs.title: Quick start
docs.text:
uv tool install adoy
adoy init my-site
cd my-site
adoy serve

→ Getting Started

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: adoy is configured through adoy.toml in the project root.

docs.title: Site
docs.text:
[site]
title = "My Site"
subtitle = "A site built with adoy"
description = "My personal site"
url = "https://example.com"
base_path = "/"
Field Description title Site name — available in templates as {{ site.title }} subtitle Short tagline description Used in meta tags url Canonical base URL, used in sitemap.xml base_path URL prefix for subdirectory deployments (e.g. "/my-repo/" for GitHub Pages)

All [site] fields are available in every template via the site global.

docs.title: Build
docs.text:
[build]
paginate = 10
timezone = "UTC"
Field Description paginate Number of items per page on listing pages timezone Timezone used when parsing dates without an explicit offset

docs.title: Template
docs.text:
[template]
default = "post.html"
base = "base.html"
Field Description default Fallback template when no section-specific template is configured base The base layout template that all other templates extend

docs.title: Paths
docs.text:
[paths]
content = "content/"
templates = "templates/"
static = "static/"
output = "public/"

All paths are relative to the project root. The defaults shown above apply if [paths] is omitted.

docs.title: Content sections
docs.text:
Each section groups content files that share a template and permalink pattern.

[content.blog]
section = "blog"
template = "post.html"
permalink = "/blog/:slug"

[content.projects]
section = "projects"
template = "project.html"
permalink = "/projects/:slug"

Files under content/<section>/ are built using that section's template and permalink.

docs.title: Permalink variables
docs.text: Variable Resolves to :slug slug field from front matter :section Section directory name :year Four-digit year from date :month Zero-padded month from date :day Zero-padded day from date :title title lowercased, spaces replaced with -

docs.title: Taxonomies
docs.text:
Taxonomies aggregate content across one or more sections.

[taxonomy.tags]
name = "tags"
path = "/tags"
applies_to = ["blog", "projects"]
sort_by = "date"
template_taxonomy = "tags.html"
template_term = "tag.html"
Field Description name Taxonomy identifier — must match the front matter field name path URL path for the taxonomy overview page applies_to Sections this taxonomy indexes sort_by How to sort pages within a term ("date" is the only supported value) template_taxonomy Template for the overview page listing all terms template_term Template for individual term pages

docs.title: Standalone pages
docs.text:
One-off pages with no section and no taxonomy indexing.

[pages.about]
template = "about.html"
permalink = "/about"

[pages.contact]
template = "contact.html"
permalink = "/contact"

The content file for a standalone page must be placed at content/<name>.md.

docs.title: File layout
docs.text:
Content files are Markdown (.md) with a YAML front matter block. They live under content/:

content/
├── blog/
│   ├── hello-world.md
│   └── second-post.md
├── projects/
│   └── my-project.md
└── about.md              # standalone page

Files directly under a section directory (e.g. content/blog/) are built as section content. Files at the top level of content/ are built as standalone pages if they are declared in [pages.*] in adoy.toml.

docs.title: Front matter
docs.text:
Every content file requires a YAML front matter block:

---
title: Hello World
description: A brief introduction to adoy.
summary: Getting started with adoy.
date: 2026-06-10
content_type: blog
slug: hello-world
status: active
tags:
  - adoy
  - tutorial
---

Body text in Markdown goes here.

docs.title: Required fields
docs.text: Field Type Description title string Page title description string Short description, used in meta tags summary string One-line summary for listing pages date YYYY-MM-DD Publication date content_type string Must match the section name in adoy.toml slug string URL identifier used in the permalink status string active, draft, or archive

docs.title: Status
docs.text: Value Behaviour active Built and included in the site draft Skipped — no output file, no error archive Skipped — no output file, no error

docs.title: Custom fields
docs.text:
Any additional front matter field is preserved and available in templates as an attribute on the page object:

cover_image: /static/images/hero.jpg
author: Jane

<img src="{{ page.cover_image }}" alt="">
<span>{{ page.author }}</span>

docs.title: Taxonomy fields
docs.text:
Taxonomy terms are declared as YAML lists using the taxonomy name as the key:

tags:
  - python
  - tutorial
series:
  - building-adoy

The taxonomy must be defined in adoy.toml under [taxonomy.*] and must list the content's section in applies_to.

docs.title: Adding a new content section
docs.text:
1. Create the section directory:

bash mkdir content/notes

  1. Add the section to adoy.toml:

toml [content.notes] section = "notes" template = "note.html" permalink = "/notes/:slug"

  1. Create the template at templates/note.html.

  2. Create content files under content/notes/.

docs.title: Standalone pages
docs.text:
Standalone pages sit outside the section system — they have no taxonomy and are not listed in any index.

  1. Create content/about.md with front matter.
  2. Declare the page in adoy.toml:

toml [pages.about] template = "about.html" permalink = "/about"

  1. Create templates/about.html.

docs.title: Markdown
docs.text: The body is rendered to HTML using mistune. Standard CommonMark syntax is supported: headings, bold, italic, links, images, lists, blockquotes, and fenced code blocks.

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
adoy requires Python 3.10 or later. Install it with uv:

uv tool install adoy

Or add it to a project:

uv add adoy

docs.title: Creating a project
docs.text:
adoy init my-site
cd my-site

This creates the following structure:

my-site/
├── adoy.toml          # site configuration
├── CLAUDE.md          # agent instructions for this project
├── content/
│   └── blog/
│       └── hello-world.md
├── templates/
│   ├── base.html
│   ├── post.html
│   ├── tags.html
│   └── tag.html
├── static/            # assets copied as-is to public/static/
└── .gitignore

docs.title: Building the site
docs.text:
adoy build

Output is written to public/. The first build is always a full build. Subsequent builds are incremental — only changed files are rebuilt.

adoy build --clean-build   # force a full rebuild

docs.title: Local development
docs.text:
adoy serve

Starts a local server at http://localhost:8000 and watches for changes. Any edit to content/, templates/, or adoy.toml triggers a rebuild automatically.

adoy serve --port 3000     # use a different port

docs.title: Next steps
docs.text:
• Configuration — site metadata, paths, content sections, taxonomies
  • Content — writing posts, managing sections and front matter
  • Theming — templates, Tailwind CSS, customising the design

docs.title: Theming
docs.text:
adoy has no built-in theme system. A theme is CSS (and optional JS) loaded by templates/base.html, with assets stored in static/. Because every page template extends base.html, changes there propagate everywhere.

templates/base.html    ← single theming entry point
static/                ← CSS, fonts, images — copied to public/static/ on build

docs.title: Template structure
docs.text:
All templates use Jinja2. Page templates must extend base.html and fill in its blocks:

{% extends "base.html" %}
{% block title %}{{ page.title }}{% endblock %}
{% block content %}
  <h1>{{ page.title }}</h1>
  {{ content }}
{% endblock %}

base.html defines two blocks:

Block Purpose title Content of the <title> tag (the base appends — {{ site.title }}) title_suffix Override to suppress or change the site name suffix content Main page body

docs.title: All templates
docs.text: Variable Type Description site SiteConfig Site-wide config from adoy.toml — site.title, site.url, site.base_path, etc.

docs.title: Content and standalone page templates
docs.text: Variable Type Description page FrontMatter Parsed front matter. Core fields plus any custom fields. content str Body rendered to HTML

docs.title: Taxonomy overview templates
docs.text: Variable Type Description taxonomy str Taxonomy name (e.g. "tags") terms dict[str, list[FrontMatter]] Term name → pages carrying that term

docs.title: Taxonomy term templates
docs.text: Variable Type Description taxonomy str Taxonomy name term str This term (e.g. "python") pages list[FrontMatter] Pages carrying this term, sorted newest first

docs.title: Tailwind CSS
docs.text: Tailwind is the recommended approach. All visual decisions live as utility classes directly in the templates, which makes the design easy to read and adjust without a separate stylesheet.

docs.title: Quick start — CDN
docs.text:
No build step required. Add one script tag to base.html:

<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
  <meta charset="UTF-8">
  <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1">
  <title>{% block title %}{% endblock %}{% block title_suffix %} — {{ site.title }}{% endblock %}</title>
  <script src="https://cdn.tailwindcss.com"></script>
</head>
<body class="bg-white text-gray-900 antialiased">
  <div class="max-w-2xl mx-auto px-4 py-12">
    {% block content %}{% endblock %}
  </div>
</body>
</html>

Use the prose class from the bundled Typography plugin to style Markdown-rendered HTML:

<div class="prose prose-gray max-w-none">{{ content }}</div>

docs.title: Production — Tailwind CLI
docs.text:
The CDN Play build is not suitable for production (large bundle, no purging). Use the standalone Tailwind CLI — no Node.js required:

# Download (Linux x64)
curl -sLO https://github.com/tailwindlabs/tailwindcss/releases/latest/download/tailwindcss-linux-x64
chmod +x tailwindcss-linux-x64 && mv tailwindcss-linux-x64 tailwindcss

# Generate a purged CSS file
./tailwindcss -i /dev/null -o static/style.css --content "templates/**/*.html" --minify

Replace the CDN <script> tag with:

<link rel="stylesheet" href="{{ site.base_path }}static/style.css">

docs.title: Common design adjustments
docs.text: Intent Element Change Wider layout <div> in <body> max-w-2xl → max-w-4xl Narrower, book-like <div> in <body> max-w-2xl → max-w-prose Dark background <body> bg-white text-gray-900 → bg-gray-950 text-gray-100 Auto dark mode <html> + <body> Add dark class to <html>; use dark: variants on <body> Serif body font <body> Add font-serif Monospace aesthetic <body> Add font-mono Larger text <body> Add text-lg

docs.title: Adding a navigation bar
docs.text:
Insert above {% block content %} in base.html:

<nav class="flex gap-6 mb-10 text-sm font-medium">
  <a href="{{ site.base_path }}" class="hover:underline">Home</a>
  <a href="{{ site.base_path }}blog" class="hover:underline">Blog</a>
  <a href="{{ site.base_path }}about" class="hover:underline">About</a>
</nav>

docs.title: Static assets
docs.text:
Files in static/ are copied to public/static/ on every build. Reference them in templates using site.base_path as a prefix so subdirectory deployments work correctly:

<link rel="stylesheet" href="{{ site.base_path }}static/style.css">
<img src="{{ site.base_path }}static/images/logo.png" alt="Logo">
